coredata.dc:title: Experimental thermal hazard investigation on carbonate electrolytes using a cone calorimeter
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Que
Lu, Song
Liu, Xiaozhao
Zheng, Kaihui
Xu, Dengji
Liu, Changcheng
coredata.dc:description: During the thermal runaway (TR) processing of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), electrolyte would release most of the heat as the main component. Thus, study on the fire risk of electrolyte is conductive to quantitatively evaluate the safety of LIBs. In this work, the combustion characteristics of three kinds of carbonate mixture electrolytes and the composition of residue were investigated. The significant parameters from the cone calorimeter tests reflecting the thermal hazards such as heat release rate (HRR), mass loss rate (MLR) and open cup flash point were measured. The HRR curves at the radiant power of 15 and 25 kW/m2 were more gradual than those at 35 kW/m2 which had one or two apparent peaks. The addition of diethyl carbonate (DEC) increased the HRRs significantly. The applicability of thermal chemistry (TC) theory used to calculate HRR in previous works was studied. The results of TC technique had a disparity with those from the cone calorimeter called oxygen consumption (OC) calorimetry especially for the electrolyte containing DEC, which was proved by thermogravimetry-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (TG-FTIR) experimental results, while it is different from the conclusion reported in previous study. The constituent of carbonate electrolytes residue was analyzed by the X-ray Fluorescence (XRF). Element fluorine (F) accounted for over 90% of the residue. The open cup flash point and ignition temperature were determined by the open cup flash point apparatus. The results showed that the fire point was very close to the open cup flash point, and the difference was within 0.1 K.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Electrolyte
Cone calorimeter
Heat release rate

Open cup flash point and ignition temperature
The comparison of two heat release rate measurement methods

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the experimental platform including a cone calorimeter.
Combustion processing of EC/EMC electrolyte mixture at a radiation power of 35 kW/m2, (a) stable combustion stage, (b) extinguishment stage.
The HRR curves of the three electrolytes at a radiation power of 15 kW/m2.
The HRR curves of the three electrolytes at the radiation power of 15, 25, and 35 kW/m2.
The HRR curves calculated by two methods TC theory and OC calorimetry of EC/EMC electrolyte at different radiation powers.
The HRR curves calculated by two methods TC theory and OC calorimetry of EC/DEC/PC electrolyte at different radiation powers.
The HRR curves calculated by two methods TC theory and OC calorimetry of EC/DEC/EMC electrolytes at different radiation powers.
Absorption peak intensity at 2350 cm-1 of three electrolyte blends with the change of temperature.
The thermal properties of different carbonate solvents.
The composition and enthalpy of formation for different electrolytes.
The calibration results of three samples measured by the open cup flash point instrument, at the test mode a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The calibration values and tests at 101 kPa.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cone calorimeter results for the three electrolytes.
The THRs calculated by the heat of combustion, using TC theory and OC calorimetry methods.

head.ce:title: Experimental thermal hazard investigation on carbonate electrolytes using a cone calorimeter

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Energy Materials and Systems

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: During the thermal runaway (TR) processing of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), electrolyte would release most of the heat as the main component. Thus, study on the fire risk of electrolyte is conductive to quantitatively evaluate the safety of LIBs. In this work, the combustion characteristics of three kinds of carbonate mixture electrolytes and the composition of residue were investigated. The significant parameters from the cone calorimeter tests reflecting the thermal hazards such as heat release rate (HRR), mass loss rate (MLR) and open cup flash point were measured. The HRR curves at the radiant power of 15 and 25 kW/m2 were more gradual than those at 35 kW/m2 which had one or two apparent peaks. The addition of diethyl carbonate (DEC) increased the HRRs significantly. The applicability of thermal chemistry (TC) theory used to calculate HRR in previous works was studied. The results of TC technique had a disparity with those from the cone calorimeter called oxygen consumption (OC) calorimetry especially for the electrolyte containing DEC, which was proved by thermogravimetry-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (TG-FTIR) experimental results, while it is different from the conclusion reported in previous study. The constituent of carbonate electrolytes residue was analyzed by the X-ray Fluorescence (XRF). Element fluorine (F) accounted for over 90% of the residue. The open cup flash point and ignition temperature were determined by the open cup flash point apparatus. The results showed that the fire point was very close to the open cup flash point, and the difference was within 0.1 K.

The reason why heat release rate (HRR) of electrolyte blends containing diethyl carbonate (DEC) calculated by thermal chemistry (TC) theory existing errors when comparing with that from oxygen consumption (OC) calorimetry is that DEC promoted CO2 production through internal chemical reactions, while TC method regards all the CO2 production are from combustion during the heating processing. Thus, OC calorimetry is more appropriate than TC theory when calculating HRR of electrolyte mixtures containing DEC. The illustration is as presented in Figure GA. Figure GA. The mechanism of CO2 production influence on errors between TC theory and OC calorimetry.
Image 1

ce:def-description.ce:para: peak value of heat release rate

ce:section.ce:para: With the characteristics of high-energy density, long-period, high voltage, and little pollution to environment, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are widely applied in every walk of life. Thereupon, the relevant fire safety problems of LIBs attract great attention [1–4]. Electrolyte is one of the main combustibles in LIBs. In the combustion processing of LIBs, a large amount of heat (more than 40%) is released from the combustion of electrolyte [5,6]. Once LIBs cause fire, the combustion action is fierce with the jet of combustibles, which would accelerate the spreading of fire and generate hazardous gas such as hydrogen fluoride (HF) [7,8]. According to previous researches, the initial decomposition temperature of electrolyte was as low as 70 °C [9–12] and the electrode materials could also react with electrolyte [13–15]. For example, Feng et al. [16] experimented with a group of six 25Ah square soft package LIBs with Li[NixCoyMnz]O2 (NCM)/graphite as its positive electrodes. The propagation characteristics of the thermal runaway (TR) processing were investigated by the heat transfer analysis. TR phenomenon is mainly caused by the violent temperature rise inside LIBs, which could be induced by short-circuit, overcharging and high-rate charging or discharging, and it is also related to the cathode material and oxygen released from inside reactions. Moreover, the highest temperature inside the LIBs was acquired. The results showed that the primary method of heat transfer in LIBs was the heat conduction. However, the significant parameters such as heat release rate (HRR) and total heat release (THR) were not measured. Zhang et al. [17] and Fu et al. [18] used a cone calorimeter to measure the HRR of several types of conventional carbonate electrolytes.

Meanwhile, another thermal chemistry (TC) theory method based on stoichiometry which is differentiated from the oxygen consumption (OC) calorimetry for HRR calculation was adopted. The results indicated the measurement of HRR by cone calorimeter was relatively appropriate. Fu et al. [7] investigated the combustion behavior of 18650 LIBs at the specific radiant power, and gained the key parameter HRR. Although the 18650 LIBs burned vigorously, the number of combustible materials such as electrolyte in a single LIB was limited. While in most cases a group of LIBs was used simultaneously, the actual fire risk could not be analyzed from the data of HRR from an individual cell. For example, Lyon et al. [19] put forward a new bomb calorimeter method to analyze the released heat from the 18650 LIBs TR processing. In previous work, THR was composed by two parts. One was the stored electric energy in LIBs; while the other was the heat release of mixture and chemical reaction [11,12,19]. Nonetheless, the HRR was not assessed. Researchers studied the behaviors of a lithium salt in dimethyl carbonate (DMC), diethyl carbonate (DEC) and ionic liquid-based electrolytes and the action mechanism of some additives, but the safety analysis was lacking [20–23].

In the current work, three types of carbonate electrolyte blends were prepared at different compositions. The thermal risk of three kinds of samples was studied at three different radiant intensities using a cone calorimeter. At first, the open cup flash point and the ignition temperature of three different electrolytes were measured by an open cup flash point tester. The effect of radiation intensities on the combustion processing was investigated. Second, several parameters, such as HRR and THR, were measured by the cone calorimeter at different radiation powers. The data of HRR calculated by the OC calorimetry was compared to the TC theory to establish a reliable process. In the previous article, Zhang et al. [17] put forward that the applicability of TC theory and OC calorimetry used to calculate HRR was equivalent. However, in this work, the key difference is that TC theory was not applicable for the HRR calculation of electrolyte containing DEC. Since TC method is based on the theory that all the CO2 was generated from the complete combustion of samples, while as for electrolyte with DEC, according to the thermogravimetry-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (TG-FTIR) experimental results, DEC would be thermal decomposed to produce CO2 by itself, and the possible reactions and mechanisms were proposed. Finally, the constituent of combustion products was analyzed by X-ray Fluorescence (XRF).

Three standard electrolyte mixtures were produced by mixing lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6) and carbonate solvents. Different solvents in the study were shown in Table 1 . The proportion of the mixture was decided based on previous studies [24,25]. Table 2 shows a definite composition of the electrolyte samples. TC theory was used to verify the HRR measured by the cone calorimeter. While the exact chemical composition of bunkers was necessary for the TC theory, these three kinds of mixtures were assumed to be compounds whose molecular formula and enthalpy of formation was calculated by the inner carbonates and the amount of substance. The calculation method to obtain molecular formula could be found in the previous study [17]. These three carbonate solvent mixtures were produced and commercially available from Tianjin Jinniu Co. Ltd.

The flash point of three mixtures was determined by an open cup flash point apparatus (SCKB3000, Zibo Shengkang Dianqi CO., LTD, China). A sample volume of 30 mL was used for each measurement. Each sample measurement was taken three times to obtain more accurate results, and the environmental temperature was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cone calorimeter and X-ray Fluorescence instrument
ce:section.ce:para: The cone calorimeter is one of the essential small size measuring apparatus in the field of fire risk [26]. Fig. 1 shows the schematic diagram of the instrument. Cone calorimeter experiments were performed at radiation power of 15, 25 and 35 kW/m2. Each sample was tested three times. The thickness of sample was 2.2 mm, and the volume was 25 mL.

Two K-type thermocouples were placed 1 and 6 cm higher than the receptacle's upper surface to detect the flame temperature. The specimen temperatures were recorded by a personal computer. From International Organization for Standardization (ISO) 5660 [26,27], the HRR
q ˙
( t )
is calculated from Equation (1):
(1)
q ˙
( t )
=
(
Δ
h c
/
r 0
)
( 1.10 )
C
Δ p
T e
⋅
X
O 2
0
−
X
O 2
1.105 − 1.5
X
O 2
where,
Δ
h c
/
r 0
for the specimen is (13.1 × 103) kJ/kg unless a more accurate value is known.
C is the orifice flow meter calibration constant.
Δ p
is the orifice meter pressure differential.
T e
is the absolute temperature of the gas at the orifice meter.
X
O 2
0
is the average value of the mole fraction of oxygen measured during the 1-min baseline measurements.
X
O 2
is the mole fraction of oxygen measured by the oxygen analyzer.
The mass variation in the processing of combustion was recorded by an electrical scale with an accuracy of 0.01 g. The composition of combustion products was analyzed by an XRF-1800.

TG-FTIR measurement was conducted on an STA 8000 simultaneous thermal analyzer coupled with an FTIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Massachusetts, America). The heating rate of TG was 10 K/min, from room temperature (approximate 25 °C) to 300 °C. The experiments were conducted with an atmosphere of nitrogen. Each measurement required 15 mL sample. The density of each sample has little difference; therefore, by using the intensity of CO2 absorption peak, the CO2 production could be estimated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Open cup flash point and ignition temperature
ce:section.ce:para: The calibration results of three samples measured by the open cup flash point instrument are listed in Table 3 .

A series of experiments were conducted by the Chinese standard “Petroleum products-Determination of flash and fire points-Cleveland open cup method” (GB/T3536-2008) for the measurement of open cup flash point for carbonate electrolytes without pretreatment at standard atmospheric pressure as 101 kPa. From the calibration results, the open cup flash point of ethylene carbonate/DEC/propylene carbonate (EC/DEC/PC) was the highest. The addition of DEC and Ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC) obviously decreased the open cup flash points of the samples. Besides, the results showed that the ignition point was very close to the open cup flash point for all the three samples.

The combustion processes of the three samples have some common characteristics as shown in Fig. 2 . During the combustion processing, three samples were ignited immediately. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the flame height was about 30 cm in the stable combustion stage, and the flame duration was about 4 min. As seen in Fig. 2(b), compared to some traditional fuel such as gasoline, a larger mass of off-white smoke was produced after the flame extinguishing which indicated that incomplete combustion occurred in this stage. There was purple and red flame during the combustion of EC/EMC and EC/DEC/EMC, while the flame with such color did not appear for EC/DEC/PC, which was because the PC in EC/DEC/PC influenced the flame reaction of lithium fluoride (LiF).

From the detection results of ignition residue, because of the limitation of instruments, the content of element Li could not be measured. Beyond that, the content of element F was the most abundant, whose average proportion was over 96.00%. The content of F in the residue of EC/DEC/PC at the radiation power of 35 kW/m2 was the highest, at 99.20%. When the radiation changed to 15 kW/m2, the content of F was decreased to 91.91%. In the case of element P, while the content of P was not high, whose average proportion was about 2.80%. The highest percentage was 6.59% in the residue of EC/DEC/PC at the radiation power of 15 kW/m2. When the radiation was 35 kW/m2, the lowest proportion was 0.52%. The content of element C was low, whose average percentage was 0.46%. After the combustion of EC/DEC/EMC at the radiation power of 15 kW/m2, the content was the highest at 1.26%. On the contrary, the lowest content was 0.18% in the residue of EC/DEC/PC at the radiation power of 35 kW/m2. The element Fe ranged the fourth, while its average content was below 0.30%. Besides, elements Si, Cr, Ni, Ca, Mn, S, Zr, Ti, Co, and Al were also detected by XRF-1800. However, their contents were quite low, and all the average proportions were under 0.1%.

Fig. 3 shows the HRR and THR curves of the three electrolytes at the radiation power of 15 kW/m2. There was only one peak shown in the curve of EC/EMC, which was followed by a long step. Earlier researchers have shown two apparent peaks in the HRR curve of binary mixture [17]. Because of the larger enthalpy of vaporization and higher flash point, the peak belonging to EC always appears in the later period of combustion. Therefore, the first peak of EC/EMC could be regarded as the main contribution by EMC, while EC made most of the second step. In terms of the peak value, the first one was two times of the latter, which indicated that the fire risk of EMC was higher than that of EC to a certain extent. As for the other two ternary mixture, both of them had three peaks. Besides, according to the enthalpy of vaporization and open cup flash point, the affiliation of three EC/DEC/PC peaks was approximately speculated to be DEC, PC and EC. With regard to EC/DEC/EMC, the first and second peaks were mainly supported by EMC and DEC, while the third one belonged to EC.

Fig. 4 shows the effect of radiation power on HRR curves of three electrolytes. Table 4 presents the parameters such as the time to ignition (TTI) and the duration of flame. The fire performance index (FPI), which is the ratio between TTI and the peak value of heat release rate (pHRR), and the fire growth index (FGI), which is the ratio between pHRR and the time to pHRR, have been suggested to be two parameters related to the flame resistance performance [28,29]. The FPI value is mainly used to evaluate the risk of flashover, while the FGI value could be applied to appraise the fire development of the materials [30]. In summary, at a higher radiation power, the peak value of HRR changed. As for EC/EMC, when the radiation power was 35 kW/m2, the value of the first peak increased remarkably, while the second step was lower than what the HRR measured at a lower radiation power. The increase in the peak was due to part of EC burned at a higher radiation power. From the data shown in Fig. 4 and Table 4, because the TTI was shortened sharply, the FPI of EC/EMC was quite low at the radiation power of 25 kW/m2, which indicated that the intensity of fire risk increased to 275% of that observed under 15 kW/m2 radiation intensity. When reflecting on FGI, because the time to reach the peak was delayed, the difference between FGI's at different radiation powers was small, which illustrated that the fire hazard showed little change with the increase of radiation power.

In case of EC/DEC/PC, when the radiation power reached to 35 kW/m2, the value of the second peak raised, and even exceeded that of the first peak. Based on the previous speculation, the first peak was mainly contributed by DEC. At the radiation power of 35 kW/m2, the first peak was almost the same as that of a lower radiation power. Therefore, it could be inferred that the growth of the second peak was due to the same amount of heat released by PC and EC in a shorter time at a relatively higher radiation power. As for FPI, similar to EC/EMC, the fire risk increased significantly when the radiation power rose to 25 kW/m2, which has no difference from that of 35 kW/m2. However, FGI experienced a substantial decrease in the radiation power of 35 kW/m2. But as the first peak of the HRR was almost the same as that of lower radiation powers, the larger second peak was the leading cause for the decrease of FGI. However, the speed of fire development of EC/DEC/PC mixture did not decrease. The extra peak might increase the fire hazard of EC/DEC/PC mixture to some degree.

In the EC/DEC/EMC case, the second peak value appeared to be grown substantially at the radiation power of 25 kW/m2, and the original three small peaks changed to two while one was big and another was small. The smaller one appeared at the beginning, which was primarily contributed by EMC because of the lowest open cup flash point. With the increase in radiation power to 35 kW/m2, the second peak appeared at an earlier point of time, and the peak value was significantly increased. As per the variation of fire risk, the FPI of EC/DEC/EMC decreased continuously with the increase of radiation power, which indicated that it was easy to reach a flashover status in a real fire condition. Moreover, the growing FGI led to a more rapid development of fire. According to Petrella's research [30], materials whose FPI values less than 0.1 would lead to a high flashover propensity. Hence, all three kinds of electrolytes at all radiation powers would have a high risk of flashover. Taken together, the fire hazard of three electrolytes increased with the gain of radiation power. And among them, the fire risk and the growth rate of EC/DEC/EMC were high.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The comparison of two heat release rate measurement methods
ce:section.ce:para:
Apart from the OC calorimetry for HRR calculation, the TC theory is also widely applied for the materials whose chemical constitution is known [31–35]. In previous research, some scholars regarded that the thermal decomposition by heating LIBs carbonate electrolytes would generate a certain amount of CO2 [36,37]. However, not all the CO2 generation is from the combustion, so the OC calorimetry for HRR measurement is likely to have some deviation. According to Equation (1), the generation of CO2 mainly influences the accuracy of
x
O 2
0
, whose variation has less effect on the results. In Enright's paper [31], the HRR calculation equation without the measurement of CO2 concentration is provided by Equation (2):
(2)
q ˙
=
Δ
h c
r 0
⋅
M
O 2
M a
⋅
φ
1 + φ
(
α − 1
)
⋅
x
O 2
0
( 1 −
x
C
O 2
0
−
x
H 2
o
0
)
⋅
m e
˙
where, α is expansion factor.
x
H 2
o
0
is the mole fraction of water vapor in the air.
φ
is the oxygen depletion factor and determined by Equation (3).
(3)
φ =
x
O 2
0
−
x
O 2
x
O 2
0
( 1 −
x
O 2
)

In the condition of considering CO2, only the calculation of
φ
changes, and its definition is as Equation (4):
(4)
φ =
x
O 2
0
( 1 −
x
C
O 2
)
−

O 2
( 1 −

0
)

−

C
O 2
)
where,
x
C
O 2
0
is the mole fraction of CO2 in environment, and
x
C
O 2
is that measured by gas analyzer.

Either in environmental or detected gas, the mole fraction of CO2 is relatively smaller than that of O2. Therefore, a small amount of CO2 would not influence much on the measurement of HRR, which was proved in earlier studies [17]. In the previous works [36,37], where massive LIBs were applied, HRR went awry because of some positive electrode materials like lithium cobalt oxides (LCO), which would generate O2 in the combustion processing. Besides, the deviation was likely to reach to 13% [7].

In this work, the TC theory from earlier study was used to calculate HRR [17]. Based on Hess's law and Lavoisier–Laplace law, the heat released during the combustion processing could be expressed as the difference value of the enthalpy of formation of the products and reactants, as shown in Equation (5):
(5)
− Q = ∑
n i
⋅ Δ
H
f , i
− ∑
n j
⋅ Δ
H
f , j
where, the subscript i represents product and j represents reactant. For a certain combustion reaction, the reaction equation could be easily obtained from stoichiometry as shown in Equation (6):
(6)
aC x H y O z + 1O2 → mCO2 + bCO + cH2O
Where, m is the amount of CO2 divided by the decrement of O2.

a, b, and c are the amount of fuel, CO, and H2O, respectively. The values of a, b, and c could be calculated by the following Equation (7) according to the conservation of atomic number before and after the reaction:
(7)
{
a =
( m − 2 )
/
( z − x − 0.5 y )
b = a x − m
c = a − 0.5 y

Equation (5) could be written with consideration of Equation (6) to obtain Equation (8):
(8)
− Q = m ⋅ Δ
H
f , C
O 2
+ b ⋅ Δ
H
f , C O
+ c ⋅ Δ
H
f ,
H 2
O
− a ⋅ Δ
H
f ,
C x
H y
O z
where, Q is the amount of heat released when 1 mol O2 is consumed. Then the HRR could be described as Equation (9):
(9)
q ˙
=
n
O 2
⋅ Q
S
=
m ˙
C
O 2
44 m
⋅
Q S
where,
n
O 2
is the mole number of the decrement of O2.
S is the area of the sample container.
m ˙
C
O 2
is the mass flow rate of CO2 in the exhaust gases which could be obtained from the results of the cone calorimeter.

As shown in Fig. 5 , in the EC/EMC mixture, there was no significant difference between both the two methods TC and OC when the peak values were compared. The pHRR acquired by TC theory was about 5% higher than that by the OC calorimetry, while the other part inosculated well and was almost the same. The radiation power did not affect the difference between pHRRs obtained by both two methods. From this, it could be concluded that the HRR results of the cone calorimeter were reliable for the EC/EMC electrolyte. According to the work by Takeuchi et al. [38], there was a reaction between EMC, DEC, and DMC:
(10) DEC + DMC↔2EMC

However, the catalyst is necessary for the occurrence of this reaction. Based on the research results, with the existence of LiPF6, there was no transesterification. Consequently, the reaction was regarded as that it would not occur in this study here. Therefore, there were only EC and EMC in EC/EMC electrolyte. Based on previous analysis, the first peak HRR of EC/EMC mixture was mainly contributed by EMC, while a portion of EC took part in the combustion processing. Similar kinds of conclusions were drawn in previous studies of EC/DMC mixture [17] and EC/EMC mixture [10].

The comparison of HRR results for the EC/PC/DEC electrolyte are shown in Fig. 6 , and it could be found that the results of the two methods had significant differences. However, the gap between the two methods tended to keep stable with the increase in radiation power. The difference in pHRRs decreased from 57 kW/m2 at a radiation power of 15 kW/m2 to 55 kW/m2 at a radiation power of 35 kW/m2, which was not significant. The error between the two methods was about 18.3%.

The HRR results of the EC/DEC/EMC electrolyte are indicated in Fig. 7 . The HRR values of the two methods diverged even more. The pHRR of TC theory at 15 kW/m2 was 110 kW/m2 higher than that of OC calorimetry and the difference increased to 147 kW/m2 at 25 kW/m2, 206 kW/m2 at 35 kW/m2.

The THR was introduced to evaluate the veracity of the HRR results by the two methods. As the heat of EMC combustion was not available, it was assumed that the heat of combustion from EMC was the same as that of DEC, the largest one among the four solvents. The combustion heat of the three electrolytes was easily calculated by averaging their components, which had the same mass percent. Then according to the mass loss, the heat of combustion under an ideal condition could be calculated as listed in Table 5 . The results indicated that the THRs of EC/DEC/EMC by TC theory were higher than those under ideal conditions, which also demonstrated that the HRR results of TC theory were higher than the actual values. Based on the study by Zhang et al. [17], the combustion efficiencies of EC/DMC and EC/DEC/DMC were 86.8% and 84.2%, respectively, which were very similar to the results observed in the current study by OC calorimetry (82.1% EC/DMC and 77.0% EC/DEC/DMC).

In previous study, the chemical reactions of LiPF6 in carbonate solutions were explored and the corresponding thermal decomposition mechanisms were proposed as follows [11],
1.
Lithium salt would be thermal decomposed to produce PF5: LiPF6 ↔ LiF + PF5;
2.
PF5 could react with water to generate HF: PF5 + H2O → PF3O +2HF.

According to the research of Kawamura et al. [9], as for the electrolytes containing DEC, the possible chemical reactions are as below:
(11) C2H5OCOOC2H5+PH5→C2H5OCOOPH4+HF + CH2=CH2
(12) C2H5OCOOC2H5+PH5→C2H5OCOOPH4+HF + C2H5F
(13) C2H5OCOOPH4→PF3O + CO2+HF + C2H4
(14) C2H5OCOOPH4→PF3O + CO2+C2H5F
(15) C2H5OCOOPH4+HF→PF4OH + CO2+C2H5F

Thus 1 mol DEC could produce 1 mol CO2 by decomposing. In general, the decomposition of the electrolytes would indeed produce a certain quantity of CO2, which could affect the reliability of the HRR results obtained from cone calorimeter. But based on Equation (2) to Equation (4), it seemed to have little influence on the results of the OC calorimetry. Unlike the TC theory, the generation of CO2 would greatly influence the HRR results when the electrolytes contained DEC.

TG-FTIR experiments were conducted to obtain the amount of formed CO2 during the decomposition of the electrolytes. In Fig. 8 , for all the three types of electrolytes, CO2 generation was observed at the temperature of 180 °C. The lowest CO2 production was observed for EC/EMC, whereas the highest production was observed for EC/DEC/PC. The production from EC/DEC/EMC was relatively lower than that from EC/DEC/PC. The difference in CO2 production between three electrolytes explained the difference in the HRR results by TC theory and OC calorimetry tests. Onuki et al. reported that EC and DEC decomposition would produce some CO2, and about 52.0% of what came from EC [39]. However, the TG-FTIR indicated that EMC could reduce the production of CO2 from EC in an EC/EMC binary solvent.

From this study, on the premise of keeping superior electrochemical performance, several potentially useful suggestions for reducing the fire risk of LIBs are proposed [40–45]: (1) The flash point of electrolyte mixture could be predicted by using that of each component for calculation. In general, the higher flash point represents a relative higher safety. (2) As for the electrolyte without carbonate solution that would be thermal decomposed to generate CO2 such as DEC, TC theory could be applied to calculate the HRR. The lower the HRR, the lower the thermal hazard. (3) As for the electrolyte containing DEC, OC calorimetry could be used to measure the HRR. The higher the HRR, the higher the fire risk. (4) The outside heat flux would influence the pHRR, so the environmental temperature and radiation should be controlled during the LIBs storage and transportation processing. (5) Fire retardant could be considered as potential addition into electrolyte for possible reducing the fire hazard of LIBs. (6) Besides improving materials characteristics belonging LIBs, battery thermal management system (BTMS) including extinguishant is a good way to decrease fire accident from the external conditions.

The combustion behavior and significant flammability parameters of three carbonate electrolyte blends were studied at three different radiant intensities using a cone calorimeter. An open cup flash point instrument determined the flash points. The addition of DEC and EMC decreased the open cup flash points significantly. The ignition points were very close to the open cup flash points for all the three samples. The HRR curves of the three electrolytes varied with different components, while the EC/DEC/EMC electrolyte had the largest pHRR and THR and the biggest fire risk. When the external radiation power reached to 35 kW/m2, the HRR curves of the EC/EMC and the EC/DEC/PC blends appeared to have extra higher peaks. The EC/DEC/EMC mixture began to have an extra peak at the radiation power of 25 kW/m2. Through the FPI and FGI values, it could find that all the three electrolytes had a high flashover propensity and a rapid-fire development speed.

The applicability of TC theory based on stoichiometry to calculate the HRR of electrolytes was studied. The results of the two methods TC theory and the OC calorimetry were compared. The results indicated that the HRR results of EC/EMC mixtures acquired by both two methods were in good agreement with errors of less than 5.0%. However, with the addition of DEC/PC and DEC/EMC, the errors increased to 22.9% and 30.0%, respectively. The results of the OC calorimetry were very close to the THR calculated by the heat of combustion at ideal conditions. The TG-FTIR results showed that the CO2 production of the three samples was much different from each other which were consistent with the errors between OC calorimetry and TC theory. Further studies are required for the measurement of combustion products of the mixtures by thermogravimetry-gas chromatography-mass spectrometer (TG-GC-MS) or some other equipment to determine the reactions that occurred during the decomposition of DEC/EMC.

Que Huang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Funding acquisition. Song Lu: Project administration. Xiaozhao Liu: Supervision. Kaihui Zheng: Validation. Dengji Xu: Writing – original draft. Changcheng Liu: Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study has been sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No: 51906238) and Open Project Program of the State Key Laboratory of Fire Science (No. HZ2020-KF04). Also, the project was supported by Science Foundation of North University of China (No. XJJ201912), Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs (STIP) of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (No. 2019L050). The authors gratefully acknowledge these supports.
